Instead, that last incomplete snippet of music goes around and around in his head, abruptly ending mid-note and slowly fading in again from a few seconds back, an earworm. He can’t jolt himself out of it. He’s a stuck record. He can never play again.

He tries to make the right shape with his remaining fingers. His hand won’t do it. He rubs his eyes with his … his good hand. He feels rotten, hungover, dehydrated. His shirt is ruined, and his arms and chest are almost grey with muck.
He sits there, huddled, for a long while, being small and unhappy and lost. He knows he’s going to have to move eventually. He’s working his way up to it.

He looks up the corridor, eyes gradually recovering. He can see all right without his glasses, as long as he doesn’t have to do much reading. He’s in a school. There are noticeboards, banks of lockers, a rainbow mural. The place is deserted and silent. There is a dull red light coming through the windows in the classroom doors on the far side of the corridor, suggesting that the Sun is low on that side of the building, rising or setting. He has taught one-off music lessons in one or two schools, but he doesn’t recognise this one.

With some unease, he examines his bad hand. The stumps of his fingers are lumpy and uneven and have healed badly. A mass of scar tissue and scabs, and no stitches in sight. As if the digits were removed with great imprecision. Hacked off. Or bitten off? It troubles him that he can’t remember. His memory is normally so sharp and clear. He thinks he’s thinking clearly, but when he concentrates and tries to access the lost time, something in that gap pushes him back. A fierce red heat.
It occurs to him that, though his severed digits have healed very badly, they have healed. They certainly aren’t bleeding, although there’s a continual ache. How long would that take?
How much time has he lost?
What the hell happened?

Way down the corridor, in the corner of his eye, he catches a glint of light. When he turns his head to look at it, he doesn’t see anything, but when he looks away, it nags at him again. A persistent, irregular winking, like an unanswered message.
He doesn’t have the willpower not to follow it.
It leads him to the school library.
It takes time for Quinn to figure out what he needs to do, that the light doesn’t just want him here because this is a quiet, safe place. That it wants to be read to.
No, not those shelves. It wants nonfiction.

Quinn sits on a small chair at a small table and opens a sturdy hardback volume that would be almost too big for some of its intended readership to lift. It’s an encyclopedia. It’s a comfortingly straightforward read. Bright diagrams and clear language. He learns about pollination, and ancient Egypt, and how jet airliners work. There are topics in the book that he never covered in school, didn’t encounter until later in life, and so only understands at a broadsheet newspaper sort of level. He learns about the genetic code, and GPS. He knew that Pluto wasn’t a planet anymore – ‘Very well,’ he said at the time, indifferent – but now he learns that this is because it is, instead, a dwarf planet, and he learns about several other interesting dwarf planets.
He becomes, for a time, unironically engrossed. When he’s finished he closes the book, and looks at its front cover again. He feels a little better.
He doesn’t remember a word of what he just read.

‘So,’ he says, to the glint that read it all with him, over his shoulder. His throat is dry, he finds. He can barely speak. ‘Is there an encyclopedia, somewhere, which you’re in? A grown-ups’ book, I mean. I suppose not?’
But it doesn’t really know what he’s saying.

‘What are you? You seem … healthier, now.’ He furrows his brow. There’s a connection that he could make here, but he can’t quite make it. He can’t figure out that the sunbeam he’s talking to eats thoughts, memories, interesting information. And that it was very, very hungry, desperate and bedraggled. And that it is sated now.

In fact, he did make the connection, quite quickly, but it ate that. And once the thought is gone, it’s difficult to form the same thought a second time. Its tendency to eat the explanation for its own behaviour is part of what made it so difficult for the Organisation to contain, originally.
There is a document, though. Elsewhere in the world. Not exactly an encyclopedia entry, but all of U-4987’s details are pinned down there.
‘Are we done?’ Quinn asks. ‘You’ve had what you wanted?’
No obvious reaction.
‘What happened? Tell me.’
Nothing.
Quinn stands up, swaying a little. ‘Fine.’ He blinks, tired, and goes to find water.

There is nobody anywhere in the school. Moving through the empty place, at this late/early hour, feels like a petty crime. There’s no power, and no running water. The school office is poky and dimly lit, piled high with paperwork. There are two small desks, each with a beaten-up office chair. There are computers that don’t work and desk phones, one off its hook. No dial tone from either of them. In the staff room he finds an unopened bottle of water and various pairs of spare shoes. He finds some trainers that fit. They’ll have to do.

There is no one outside the front of the school, either. Yellow zigzags where parents aren’t allowed to park to drop children off, traffic-calming measures, eerie bollards the shape and size of seven-year-olds. It feels like there’s no weather; no temperature, still air, no birdsong. The sky is a clear dark red. There is a faint hiss in the air, a distant white noise like the crashing of an ocean.

He follows that sound, dragged away from the school and downhill, out of that one suburb. He passes a bus stop, which informs him what town he’s in and how to reach the city centre. He walks that way. The sound gets louder that way. He reaches a main road.

There is scattered rubbish, broken windows, old remnants of fires. There are abandoned vehicles in the streets, a few with keys in the ignition. The invitation is there to steal one, but he’s not there yet.
‘So the place has gone to hell,’ he says to himself.

Adam Quinn has hiked, and camped. He has a few immediate ideas for finding food and water, and there is limitless shelter. But he was never a survivalist. He doesn’t know what he’d do if he had to put up a fight with someone.
There are no bodies, that he can see.

He walks far enough, and for long enough, to notice that the brilliant thing hanging low in the sky to his right isn’t rising or setting. Either the world’s stopped turning completely, or it isn’t the Sun. The sound in the distance is starting to sound more like people. Thousands of voices, like a concert.

And then, after following the main road for some miles, he reaches the brow of a hill, and he has clear line of sight to what’s beyond, another quarter of a mile away, at the bottom of the valley, at the centre of the city. He sees what’s been built there, the gigantic, sprawling, windowless edifice. A sickening gulf opens up in the pit of his stomach, and he parses the sound that he’s hearing, and he knows where everybody is. He remembers what happened, what arrived. The world has not ended. It has been remade, and it is continuing in a different form.

There is a tall, shadowed entity standing beside the sarcophagus. The sarcophagus is monumental, but the shadowy thing is like a skyscraper or a radio tower, tall enough that its upper reaches fade into the dark atmosphere. Quinn only notices it on his second glance, because the thing is so incredibly thin. But the thing, Quinn then realises with a jolt, is moving. Swaying. Maybe turning. It is far away, and moving slowly. It’s not clear whether it’s alive, or whether it has a head or eyes. It would need extremely good eyes to be able to make out something as small as Quinn from that distance.

Nevertheless, Quinn runs. Back down the hill, in terror, below its field of view. (There was another one behind it, which he didn’t even notice.) After a few paces he veers to the left, into an open car door, and tries the ignition. Nothing. He gibbers. A flat battery? The next two cars he tries are dead too. He gives up, and continues to run, knees already starting to buckle. He doesn’t know if anything is truly trying to catch him, or, if they were, how they’d be tracking him, or how to hide from that, or how to defend himself.

The sound seems to follow him, a roaring in his ears, a pressure in his skull, no matter how far he flees from its source. He tries to forget about it, to put it out of his mind, to think about something else. He can’t do it anymore. It’s here now.

Miles away, he raids a hypermarket for packaged food and water. With the power out, and only the ‘sun’ coming in through the front, it’s a frighteningly ill-lit place, but he finds his way around. There’s an aisle with basic camping equipment. Elsewhere, to his infinite relief, a section with passable clothing. He finally throws his grey tatter of a shirt away. There’s a display with mobile phone handsets and prepaid SIM cards. He finds a handset with enough charge to actually turn on. It has no signal, not even for emergency calls, and no working GPS, and it doesn’t even know what the current date and time are. The thing is as useful to him as a house brick.

After assembling a backpack of gear, he finds a back room, a staff area with couches. Beaten-up but soft. He sits down, just for a second, to take stock. He experiences about an hour of deep, untroubled sleep, and ten of nightmares.
When he wakes up, his fingers are still gone, and it’s almost as bad as it was the first time.

chapters.title: 3: BLOOD/BRAIN
paragraphs: Blood/brain
There’s no day/night cycle.

A few days into his trek, Quinn realises that he can perform an experiment. He selects a building with a high ceiling to sleep in, a local library. Before turning in, he sets up a Foucault pendulum. He suspends a heavy rock by wire from the ceiling and sets it swinging. The following ‘morning,’ the slow pendulum is still swinging, and it has precessed. It’s swinging at about a right angle from the mark he made before he went to sleep.
That means the world is still spinning.

On reflection, he doesn’t know if it proves anything. It’s not clear whether the Sun or the Moon still exists, or any celestial object at all other than the red-black eye socket at the horizon. The eye never moves. It casts long, threatening shadows, while being bright enough to blind Quinn whenever he has to walk in that approximate direction, which is about half the time. Regardless of the physical evidence, it doesn’t feel as if he’s walking on a real Earth, or fully awake. He feels like an ant crawling across the face of a rough-hewn monolith, crawling into and out of the runes chiseled into the face of that monolith, runes that form an unstoppable, apocalyptic mythology. He has migraines, and there are blotchy multicoloured zigzags in his vision by the end of most ‘days.’ He feels as if the whole world is perpetually dropping away from beneath his feet, like he and it are both plummeting into an abyss.

On occasion, on the horizon, he sees a skyscraper-sized figure stalking past. He holds still, or hides, and it doesn’t see him. Other than that, the world is seemingly deserted, standing empty, like an overturned car in a muddy ditch. Open doors, lights still blinking. Quinn feels … detached. Lucky. Guilty.

There are more sarcophagi, dotted around the country. He hasn’t come within eyeshot of another one, but he can hear them from a long way out. They are in the city centres, the biggest ones, but there are others in the country, here and there. He plots long detours around them. The noise they broadcast is intolerable. The noise creates, in him, things that he dearly wants not to be flashbacks.

The sunbeam wants him to be somewhere. For lack of better ideas, he is trying to follow it. He is aware that the sunbeam is the reason he isn’t still … one of … ‘Us.’ He is overwhelmingly grateful, and not able to communicate his gratitude.

Unfortunately, the sunbeam’s navigation is erratic and unreliable. It weaves, and tries to lead him through hills, and sometimes disappears completely for up to a ‘day’ at a time. It has no conception of the paper road atlas Quinn is using, or roads in general. It just drags him in a general direction.

Quinn marks his journey on the map. There is, at least, some kind of route taking shape, an actual journey from an A to a B. He isn’t going in circles. He’s progressing. To what, he doesn’t know. He almost hopes it’s nothing. There’s a bizarre pointlessness to it. Or, maybe, an inevitability to his failure.
He goes into a local shop and, along with more packaged food and bottled water, steals a cheap digital wristwatch. It has a date function. Today is the thirtieth of April; it’s just gone lunchtime.
Time is still passing. On some level, all of this is factual. It’s happening.
And if it’s really happening, then what?

There is no longer any ambiguity about what, specifically, is happening. The world has long since passed through వ’s antimemetic boundary layer and into its radioactive core. There is no longer a need for వ to pretend that it is not what it plainly is. What else could it be? What difference could it make now, what could oppose it? It stands there in plain sight. Quinn sees it. All of conscious reality sees it. It’s happening everywhere, to everyone. It’s not physically possible to conceive of anything else.

There is no worse-case scenario than what’s happening now. There’s no race against time; there’s no ticking clock; there’s no last second, the last second was years ago. There’s nothing to avert. This is it, the final game position, the highest and most refined form of human civilisation. This is the shape of the next thousand years.
వ stands there. Monstrous, casual and indifferent.

And Quinn is alone with his thoughts for a long period of time, and has little else to think about, and he wrinkles his brow, and he blinks a long blink, and looks again, and he realises what it was that he wasn’t seeing –
వ is standing there. Like a human stands.
The sunbeam is getting brighter. Anxious, maybe.
Quinn’s body clock has wandered far out of skew. He’s been living twenty- to twenty-five-hour days. It’s technically around midnight when he first lays eyes on UO Wyeleigh.

There is a protective field surrounding it, stamped into reality by the detonation of the antimemetic warhead, radiating out a few hundred metres beyond the site’s perimetre. It’s a psychological repulsion, not a physical one. A thick bulwark of irrelevance. There’s nothing here. Just keep walking. Quinn succumbs to the effect. Thirty minutes’ walk down the road, U-4987 – Quinn doesn’t know its number, but he has decided, again, independently, to call it ‘Sunshine’ – makes him double back. This happens two more times. On the fourth pass, after triple-checking the gap on his map, he manages to make himself see the extra road, leading in the other, other direction.

After a few hundred yards, there is a tall, intimidating security fence, and a security checkpoint. The checkpoint is unattended and the gate is standing open. Quinn proceeds along the road, to the main part of the site.

There is no ancient, dramatically overgrown ruin. The containment breach that led to the installation’s destruction only happened a few years ago, and the bomb blast that concluded the outbreak was figurative, not physical. About a third of Wyeleigh’s main building has been torn down, but the rest is perfectly intact and unmarred. Mother Nature has not reclaimed it. Gnarled trees are not sprouting from the damaged side. It is, simply, an office building.

He walks all the way around it. There are several more buildings, plenty of parking, an armory (buried in rubble), a garage (intact). There’s even a pond. There is a still, safe atmosphere about the place. It’s as if Wyeleigh has its own cool microclimate. It’s quieter, easier to think. Even the light here is fractionally yellower, more natural.

The main entrance is sealed with steel doors, but Quinn is able to effect entry through the damaged side of the building, over the rubble. He moves at a medium-slow pace. He can’t afford to blunder into anything, but if he goes too slowly, he knows, he’ll overthink the situation and become scared, and have to retreat all the way out of the building. Sunshine wants him inside. There is, he can only infer, something it wants to show him.

Or someone? It occurs to him that he hasn’t seen any signage indicating whose site this is. The place is clearly intended to be secure and/or secret. It was inordinately difficult to find, even with Sunshine steering him in the right direction.
A secret organisation? A government agency?
A thought occurs to him. He says, ‘Is this … where it came from?’
After creeping a little farther into the building, torch aimed forward almost in self-defence, he adds:
‘Is this where it escaped from?’
He is figuratively, technically, close to correct.

There is a low shape in the dark. It is a corpse, seated against a corridor wall. It’s clutching a combat knife, which it seems to have buried up to the hilt in its own inner thigh, opening a gushing artery. Quinn backs up against a wall, unable to look closely at the body but equally unable to let it out of his eyesight, in case it … does something. He feels faint. It doesn’t help that at exactly that moment, the fluorescent lights in the corridor come up, triggered by his movement, giving him a much better look at the scene. The scene is about as bloody as any suicide can be.
‘No. No, thank you,’ he says. He backs up. He backs all the way up the corridor and through the ruin to the virulent red place that passes for daylight, and there he throws up.

He goes back to the pond, where there’s a bench he can sit on. He feeds Sunshine: he pulls his book out, a famous director’s autobiography, and reads another half-chapter. It’s not like normal reading, it’s a passive act, walking his eyes across the words. The words just fall into him, into blank space, snapped up and unremembered. After that, he eats something himself. He is procrastinating.

‘Is there any way at all that you can tell me what it is you’re taking me to?’ he asks Sunshine, pointlessly. ‘Just … release some of the tension? And, I don’t like to ask the seethingly obvious question, but: What is this place?’
Nothing.

In the end he goes back in because he doesn’t have an alternative. Ignore Sunshine forever? Lie down and die? He read, once, about a scientific experiment where subjects were left alone in a room with nothing but a bare metal contact. The contact, they were told, would give them an unpleasant electrical shock if they touched it. Most of the subjects, after a sufficiently long and boring wait, touched it. They received the promised unpleasant electrical shock. Quite a few of them touched it a second time.
Well, Sunshine doesn’t seem to be scared. That, he hopes, means something. He, for one, is jumpy as hell.

He finds more bodies. There aren’t as many of them as he expected, given the size of the building. Some of them are in groups, having died during violent altercations, or during more complex scenes that Quinn cannot fully parse. Some of them are dismembered, or just scattered pieces. Some of them appear to have been dead for significantly longer than the rest; they are little more than wafer-thin skin wrapped around skeletons, and there are strange things written on the walls beside them. He never works out why.

At first, nearly every door he meets is locked. But he steels his nerves, and returns to each of the deceased staff in turn, and retrieves their keys and security passes. After most of a day of work, he has the run of the place, with only a few highly secure control rooms denied to him.

The passes have names on them, and coloured stripes indicating seniority, but no company logo or anything else to indicate who the people actually worked for. The deliberate anonymity is actually becoming borderline obnoxious. Does this organisation simply have no name?
‘You fellows are evasive.’

There’s still power. There’s running water. An agency with some degree of competence, at least. An agency that builds things to endure. The people are absent, but the physical systems they built are still here, and functioning, and ready.

He finds the Passive Containment Archive. Most of the vaults are locked, some are standing open and seemingly empty. Vault 4J reportedly contains U-2200, an ancient Malaysian antimemetic demigod interred in a stone idol. But the rock is silent.

After some exploring, he discovers vault 601, which is not the largest of the vaults, but clearly the most heavily secured. Its airlock is locked tight and requires an extreme level of authority to open, an orange and red pass that Quinn does not have. The files state only that this is where U-3125 is contained. He does not know what this signifies.

Near the site entrance – he can’t figure out how to unlock the steel doors, even from the inside – he finds a security office, with printed floor plans of the whole site. He crosses off the rooms he’s visited, and the rooms that are destroyed. Everything remaining is locked. Aboveground, anyway. Underground, there are warrens of tunnels, and dozens more containment units. And, thirty floors belowground, a single incredibly large vault of unstated purpose.
This final vault draws his attention in, magnetically. This, he knows in his bones, is where Sunshine wants him to go.

As the freight elevator descends, Quinn finds that a kind of anxious pressure is building above him. The air is rapidly getting warmer, and he suddenly realises that if the elevator breaks down right now, he’ll likely be helplessly trapped, and die. He shouldn’t have used it. He should have used the emergency stairs. Too late.

The elevator lands. There’s an empty corridor. He follows it, drawn forward. There’s an airlock at the far end, a wall of white metal big enough to drive a truck through. The airlock is closed, but there are seven or eight overlapping circular holes punched through it, making a combined gap that is easily big enough to admit a human. Beyond the airlock, there is a vast dark space. Quinn has climbed through the hole and walked five paces out into the darkness before he even thinks about what he’s doing.

There are shapes out there, illuminated by the scant light falling from the airlock corridor – lumps that could be more dead people. Quinn’s own shadow blocks much of the light. He takes out his torch. It is absolutely silent down here, and the temperature is uncomfortable, making him sweat. The rest of the huge vault, as far as he can shine his light, is totally empty – but his torch is not powerful enough to illuminate a space this big, so it’s hard to be certain.

He almost trips over something in the darkness. A shiny black chunk of metal, a mass of erratic pipework well over a metre long. At one end it has what is clearly some kind of stock, and some kind of trigger. And, at the other end, two long tines. A weapon. It is, he will discover later, U-7381.
He steps over it. He advances. A loud tone is building in his ears as he gets closer. Sunshine is getting brighter, agitated, a gold-white glare in one of his eyes. He shouldn’t be here.

There are … he counts … twelve dead. Eleven of them dead in a rough circle around a twelfth, a woman lying flat on her back. Just outside of the circle, there is a military truck with the inert remains of a complex machine mounted on its back. There is a cable leading down to a control unit lying on the floor, under the hand of the dead woman. It doesn’t look quite like a ritual. It looks somewhat like a murder.
He whispers, to himself, ‘Was this where it happened?’
She has her hand on the switch.
‘Was she the one who did it? Or did she die trying to stop it?’

In her other hand she is clutching an autoinjector, a fat white pen-like device with a luminous orange vial inside it. It seems important. Gingerly, he prizes it out of her grip. Sunshine glows painfully when he tries to take a closer look, but he can see that the cap is still on the syringe, and the vial is full. If the woman intended to use it, on herself or any of the others, she failed.

Her security pass looks different from the others. It has a bright diagonal stripe across it in red and orange. He takes it. He can’t see it clearly at first; Sunshine is disturbing his vision too much now. He squints. The roaring in his ears is making it hard to think. The card says, MARIE QUINN / SITE DIRECTOR.
He stares at it, unable to say anything, for a long time.

It is, of course, a terribly commonplace name. If he stopped to gawp at every other Quinn he met, he would never get anything done. But Sunshine led him here. Pulled him out of the inferno and led him, determinedly, across an apocalypse, here. To her. Truly: what are the odds?
‘Why?’
Sunshine is losing it. It’s flickering, like it’s going to explode.
‘Who is she?’

It is an abstract being, with an uncertain beginning and end. One-of-a-kind, endling, lifecycle-less – it cannot explain itself in human terms, it cannot explain how he knew her, only how it knew her, through the memories it absorbed/ate/stole. It splits open, and those memories burst from its core, bathing Quinn in light, blinding him. Little items set down and then misplaced, earrings, teaspoons, ballpoint pens. Forgotten nooks, ill-tended plants, volumes of historical and sports trivia, crossword clues, facts and figures. Cigarette after cigarette. Moments of love and fear and burnout and anger, of duty and determination and, fleetingly, even peace; interesting word definitions, old friends’ faces and phone numbers, concerto performances, plot twists, shooting stars, rare bird sightings, foolish personal aphorisms. The memories are out of sequence. Quinn sees himself in them, everywhere. He sees himself through another pair of eyes, doing ordinary, momentary things.

Trimming his nose hairs or fumbling his way through the mortgage spreadsheet or picking out wine or just standing close, talking to her, stroking her behind the ear. Everything overlaps, everything crashes into everything else. The picture show is too much, and it’s too beautiful and too rapid to follow and it hurts to watch and he doesn’t want to watch it, but he doesn’t have a choice. There’s nothing else.

He doesn’t recognise a single image. He doesn’t remember. The show illuminates nothing, awakens nothing. It’s fiction to him, a secondhand telling, a wonderful, unbelievable myth. Someone else’s story, not his, not anymore. It’s gone. It was taken.

It’s over. Quinn finds he is on his hands and knees, eyes screwed up. Sunshine raises a despairing wail. It wants her back. There’s no way he can explain to it that neither of them can have her back, ever.

There was one particular scene in the reel. An image, flying past with a thousand others in the midst of the stream, but final, chronologically. A scene in a Humanoid Containment Unit, with the three of them. Quinn seizes on it. It’s important. To Sunshine, he says:
‘You did this to me?’
Nothing.
‘She did this to us? To protect me? What … damfool … calculus is that?’
It shares with him its baying fear, its panic. The thing was going to annihilate him. There were no other options.
‘I don’t accept that,’ he says. ‘And just how was this supposed to be better? She lost everything anyway. Everybody did.’
He scrabbles for the dropped pass again, grips it in his good fist.
The red and orange stripe. Asinine ‘seniority.’ He raises it over his head, ready to throw it at the ground, break it in half. But he hesitates for a long, long moment.

He doesn’t not believe in impossible choices, no-win scenarios. He believes that, truthfully, shit happens. He doesn’t think of himself as stupid and he doesn’t like to think he’d choose to spend his life with someone stupid and maybe, maybe he’ll accept that there was genuinely nothing else for it, and he and she and Sunshine took the only option open to them in the circumstances, and there really was no choice at all. But what he does believe is that there’s more to it. Always, or nearly always. Another level to any bloody scenario. A reason for the scenario.
He says:
‘It. Did this. To us.’

You throw the lever or don’t, hit one person with the trolley or let five other innocents die, and maybe that’s murder, and maybe that’s something that’s going to stay on your conscience or stain your soul for all eternity. But someone put you there, alone and helpless, in front of the lever. Someone set you up, someone tied those people to the tracks.

There is a containment unit that this pass, and only this pass, will open. It might all still be hopeless; he’s trying not to fool himself about that, still. But he will, at least, find out what the thing is that did this to them. To everyone.
He is owed an explanation. He already has its number.

chapters.title: 4: U-3125
paragraphs: ‘And as years pass, the manifestations will continue, growing denser and knitting together … until the whole world is drowning in them, and everybody will be screaming …’

Quinn has watched this recording multiple times now. He mouths the words along with her: ‘“Why did nobody realise what was happening?” And nobody will answer, because everybody who realised was killed, by this system …’
He skips forward.

‘Three years ago, UO Antimemetics was a mini-Organisation of more than four thousand people. Today, we are ninety people. Tomorrow it’s going to be fewer. Our original headquarters is gone. Obliterated from our collective memory. No one remembers!’
Forward.
‘There’s a machine we could build. All it would take is twenty years, a lab the size of a country, and all the money in the world.’
He skips forward again.
‘I don’t remember the last time I slept without having a nightmare about Adam –’
He stops it.
Then he plays the other recording. The recording that was recorded as a response to the first, by a very different Marie Quinn. One with no one left to lose.
She takes a second to start talking. She collects her thoughts, and does not find it easy to look at the camera.

‘I remember now. I don’t know why I remember now, but I remember that there is a disused heavy engineering lab beneath the Wyeleigh complex. Vault B30-000. I don’t know when it was built, or who built it, or what its original purpose was. No one has gone down there in living memory.

‘My belief is that there are good reasons for those gaps in my knowledge. I believe that this vault was originally constructed to house a long-term project where Dr Edward Hix would construct this machine. That machine.’ She points off-screen, at an area of wall.

Adam Quinn has already found that area of wall. He’s retrieved all of those schematics. He holds up the paperwork, demonstrating that he is following her reasoning. The machine is called an ‘irreality amplifier,’ and he doesn’t have a clue how it’s supposed to work.

Marie continues. ‘While that’s been going on, the rest of the Antimemetics Division has been fighting an unconscious war in order to buy time. We have been, inevitably, losing. But we have been losing as slowly as humanly possible.

‘Now, today, the time we have bought is up. It is, I will concede, an extremely bad sign that nobody inside B30-000 has broken the seal yet, but there is no ground left for us to cede, and there are no more bodies to throw into U-3125’s maw to slow it down. It’s here, ready or not.

‘I’m going to go to B30-000 and use the machine. I think I can get the information out through the airlock. I think I can get to the vault alive. This was the plan. It’s become garbled in the retelling because of variables, but I know that this was my plan, because I know myself. What else could it have been?’
Adam is verifying all of this against his notes. He nods.
Now comes the important part.
‘Standard procedure is that I have to tell you what to do next if this doesn’t work. That’s the asynchronous research covenant.

‘But I don’t have a good picture of who you can even be, watching this and alive. In your scenario the vault is empty, there’s no amplifier, Edward Hix is missing, I’m dead, this site is ruined, and – how would you even get in here? Are you UO? Are you conscious? Is there a single word of this that you comprehend?’
‘One or two,’ Quinn murmurs.
‘You live in a world bathed with U-3125. That’s the loss condition. I can’t help someone who doesn’t exist. Unless …’
She regards the camera for some time, lips tightened into a grim line. Then she terminates the recording.
Quinn flips through his notes one last time. He takes a deep breath, and starts a recording of his own.
‘You’ve been extremely helpful, in fact. You’ve given me the answer. I say “you,” but I’ll confess I don’t fully know for whom I’m recording this. I found your body.’
He pauses.
‘And I’m sorry.

‘… You were right. About everything. It’s here now, and what it’s done to the world is frankly unspeakable. I’m still conscious. I think. I’m walking and talking. I might be the only one. I have seen neither hide nor hair of anybody else. At least, out in the open. According to my file – yes, I’ve found the files – the neurological condition I have, which protects me from this kind of mental interference, is very rare. Not totally unique … But how many other people have this U-4987 critter on their side as well? I looked it up. And like a lot of the things in your database, it seems to be one of a kind.’

Aside, he adds, ‘I lost a lot of time in your database. That dry house style of yours makes for bizarrely compelling reading. I’m going to cautiously assert that at least half of the entries are disinformation, yes? They certainly can’t all be true at once …

‘I don’t know what’s happened to the rest of the Organisation. I found your communications room, and I’m … decently sure that I got the equipment properly operational. There were written instructions, some key frequencies to try in case of emergencies, that sort of thing. But, nothing. And I’ve been thinking about what that means. It’s possible that there are surviving Organisation staff, in bunkers somewhere. Somewhere else. If I were them, I’d be refusing incoming calls, because it seems like this thing is an idea, and we all know that an idea can travel across a phone call. But the Organisation outside of your division didn’t have your training, so I don’t know if they’d have even thought to shut themselves away when U-3125 arrived, or even if they’d have bunkers, or the right kind of bunkers. So, I’d guess that most of them were pulled into the … mess, along with everybody else.
‘Which doesn’t necessarily make them dead. Most of the people in there are alive.’ He shivers. ‘But it does mean, it’s down to me.
‘So.

‘From the evidence I can see, this was never the sole Antimemetics Division site. There were others. I imagine they’re now all effectively invisible to (most) passersby, of course, just like this one, but I presume that they, just like this one, still physically exist. Your plan, I think, was in place for longer than you know. And since you weren’t at liberty to retain its details, you put it into action more than once.

‘Your original headquarters. Your base of operations, up at UO Stanmoor, sent to oblivion years ago. I have a map of the base, here in the older files. There was a vault there, just like this one and just like B30-000. Same airlock, same, ah, what was the terminology … “broad-spectrum informational cladding.”

‘Stanmoor is a nonentity, of course. It’s likely ruined, ruined a long time before this all happened, and it’s quite definitely a long goddamned way from here. You would never have made it even if you tried. But I know precisely where it is, and I think it’s better than half a chance.

‘I could die on this jaunt too, naturally. The world outside of here is a horror show, and nothing is simple. But I’ll give it my best shot. And whomever I’m speaking to, if anybody: Well, follow me. With care. I could be dead wrong.’
He pauses the recording.
He resumes recording.
‘You saved my life,’ he says. He wipes an eye. ‘I’m still here. I wish you were here.’
He stops the recording.
‘I’m so scared,’ he says.

As he moves down the forest road away from Wyeleigh, he approaches the edge of the antimemetic crater. He squares his shoulders, preparing to reenter the presence of U-3125. His inner ear starts free-falling again. The ambient light is reddening.
‘Where were you, just now?’ someone calls out to him.

He slows, and stops. He squints into the intense light ahead of him, shielding his eyes. He can just about make out a figure standing there. The trees on each side of them rustle and move. They’re too tall. Spider-scrapers. A wave of dread hits Quinn, followed closely by one of perverse relief. In the back of his head, Sunshine trembles.
‘Why can’t I track you?’ the unidentified man says. His voice sounds faint. ‘You’re so weak, it’s like you don’t exist. I just wasted two days trying to pick you up again. What’s wrong with you?’
Quinn says nothing.

The man is closer. He didn’t walk, but the distance between them halves, and his voice is easier to hear, though he is still too bright to look at. His body structure blurs and flickers. ‘You’re not one of Them,’ he says. ‘And you’re not one of Us. And you’re sure as hell not the hero. Maybe that’s how you did it. You don’t mean anything.
‘Why are you wasting your time on this? Whatever the fuck this is. Kill yourself. It’s not going to work.’
Quinn knows that.

The light collapses. The figure smashes into focus, becoming physical. It’s a real human. A skinny twenty-something with scruffy, uncut hair and a sketchy beard. He is shirtless, and there is a deep, black pit in his clavicle, a hole where he has clearly been very badly wounded. Blood has run down his chest, soaked his jeans and forearms, and dried black. Fresh blood is still coming, building up thick layers, which shouldn’t be possible. Quinn doesn’t spot the second hole in his gut, obscured by too much blood.
Quinn is trying to keep his expression neutral, but he knows it isn’t working. He can feel his left hand, his bad hand, starting to shake. He gets a tighter grip.
A part of him still wants to ask the man why. But there is no possible answer.

It’s not going to work, Quinn tells himself. He got a fair distance, but surely the man is here to kill him now, and he will, at the instant that his curiosity ebbs. Surely, despite his protestations, the man can clearly see him, and what he’s clutching in his arms. It’s over. It was over a long time ago.
… And yet.
If he saw it, wouldn’t he have been disarmed by now? They’re at the edge of the crater. The man said he couldn’t see into the blast radius of the … memory warhead, whatever it was.
He can’t come any closer. He doesn’t comprehend.

‘This is what our race really is,’ the man explains, spreading his hands to gesture at the whole world. ‘We lied to ourselves for thousands of years. But this is it. This is how it was always meant to be.’
‘That’s –’ Quinn begins, then grunts with annoyance, raises U-7381, and fires.

In the moment that Quinn takes to raise the weapon and take aim, the man begins to react. Quinn starts to act; he starts to mean something. Quinn comes into focus for him in turn, and Red starts to perceive what Quinn stands for in this scene, if weakly. At a step removed, perhaps; what Quinn stands for standing for. At minimum, he senses the outline of the weapon, what it means. But, at that instant, Sunshine has begun to move. It arrows forward, over Quinn’s shoulder, masking his intent with its own. The gun’s gleaming points are still rising toward horizontal, wavering as they rise, but Red’s attention splits.

Sunshine is personal. It is a being of raw memories, trivial factoids, small, human moments; Red is completely impervious to its attack. He dismisses it, snuffs it out with utter callous ease, a pinch of his fingers. But this means that he is distracted, turned partially away from Quinn, when U-7381 discharges. The weapon is absolutely silent aside from the click of the trigger. It’s a good shot. The front half of Red’s head disappears, and nine-tenths of his throat, and most of one shoulder. Red’s remains twist as they fall. He lands ‘face’ down.

Quinn has to turn away quickly from his work, gasping with inarticulate disgust. There is much, much more blood than he wanted there to be. He almost drops the gun. He gets a tighter grip on it, again. He turns back, and aims back at the bloody ruin of the man’s head. He wants to throw up. He controls himself. In through the mouth, out through the nose. He’s okay. Red isn’t moving.
‘I let him talk for too long,’ he says, apologetically.
Sunshine does not respond.
Quinn turns, scanning his surroundings. There’s nothing in the corner of his eye, or the back of his mind. Sunshine is gone.
Sunshine has disappeared before. It left him alone for long stretches during the march, but … Quinn plays back those two seconds of action, realising what happened. It was so quick. Uncaring, easy.
‘Oh.’

He doesn’t know if Sunshine is dead. Organisation literature was unclear on whether the thing was able to die, or, for that matter, meaningfully alive. But it is gone. Does an idea die? Does it go somewhere?
It bought him the moment. Why?
He says, ‘I feel that I should say a few words.’
But they do not come to him.
He uses the gun to erase the rest of Red’s remains. He does this methodically, cleaning a large ellipse of the asphalt road.
He says, to the space, ‘What I was going to say was something along the lines of: “That’s a lie. That’s what you are. You’re the lie.” But, ah.’
He looks up, hearing movement in the forest all around him. He realises that the immense spider forms – he’d sincerely forgotten about them, they were standing there so quietly – are dispersing.

The atmosphere, however, is not changing. The sky is not returning to blue. There is still that red eye/star, and that heinous pressure. U-3125 remains the dominant force in the world, and Quinn’s objective remains: he must travel north, alone now, and find out what happened to the dubiously late Dr Edward Hix.

chapters.title: 5: WILD LIGHT
paragraphs: Wild Light
Twelve years earlier:
The meeting room is containment unit STM-00-1006, which is the skull of a stillborn Cryptomorpha gigantes.

The hollowed-out space inside the skull cavity is a prototypical Vegas room – a place where what happens, stays. People go in, they come out, their memories are sieved out of the universe as they leave and they remember nothing. The skull was acquired in the nineties. The information suppression effect is a byproduct of the species’ natural antimemetic camouflage, a phenomenon that rendered the colossally tall creatures somehow nearly impossible to observe in the wild. It’s a phenomenon Edward Hix and his team spent years figuring out how to replicate. They’ve got it, now. They can synthesise C. gigantes bone, extruding it in prefabricated pieces from steel grids. They can bolt the plates together to make hermetically sealed boxes. Passive memetic insulation, no need for complicated machines; it’s got a lot of potential.

The skull is forty-five metres long, sixteen wide and fifteen tall. It resides at the centre of a vast purpose-built containment unit of its own, surrounded by the rest of the same C. gigantes individual’s bones, laid out in meticulous radial patterns for space efficiency. The ossuary occupies about a third of the containment unit’s floor area. The rest comprises immense industrial vessels that hold its harvested organs. Some of them are actual vessels, repurposed cargo ships loaded with brain matter and skin tissue. The unit is by far the largest single building at the Unknown Organisation base at Stanmoor.

The floor plan of the warehouse is clear enough, navigable if grim. But from ground level, on foot, the place is a vertiginous, intimidatingly macabre place, even fluorescent-lit around the clock. Hix walks down an echoing canyon created by, on his left, a hundred-metre-long foreleg bone and, on his right, a blue steel container holding the creature’s duodenum, including the germ farm. Ahead, the skull peers down the canyon at him, a distant yellow-white tower, fuzzed with scaffolding and disused scanning rig, its eye sockets vacant black.
As he walks, Hix has to remind himself continually that these are all the remains of a single organism, one of the tiniest examples of its species.

Behind the C. gigantes skull, where there used to be the creature’s first neck vertebra, there is now a large compound mechanical airlock, a ramp and some steps, and a staging area. The staging area serves as a miniature customs desk, tracking every person and item entering and leaving STM-00-1006. Although memories are wiped on exit, written and electronic records emerging from the interior have to be handled manually. Standard procedure is for the first person exiting the room to bring written instructions for the Filtration Officer, telling them what other information from the room interior needs to be scrubbed, and what is safe to retain. Usually the list of information to retain is very short.

There are seats, scanners, a coffee machine, a trolley loaded with cleaning equipment and a stack of cages for the germs. Parked just outside of the staging area, there is also a limousine – bulletproof.
‘Where’s everybody else?’ Hix asks the Organisation clerk who meets him, whose name is Fletcher. ‘I’m not late.’

‘This way, please,’ she says, leading him to a seat near a scanner. Hix has gone through this procedure a dozen times now, so he knows to hold his left arm out. Fletcher tears the wrapper off a sterile bracelet-like sensor and clamps it around Hix’s left wrist, then studies a nearby screen. ‘They went in almost an hour ago,’ she says.
Hix frowns. That’s not usual. Why would they tell him a different start time? Why would they need an hour of preparation time before he showed up? ‘Did they say anything?’
‘Of course not.’
Hix hasn’t the slightest clue what this meeting is about, or what any of the previous meetings were about, or even if they have a common topic.

Actually, he does have some clues. The timing of the meetings is one. The first took place early this year, and when they emerged, amnesiac, they were clutching written instructions from themselves to themselves to continue meeting monthly. Around July, the meetings became weekly. They had three last week – he missed the last of them, he was on a work trip to China. And after Friday, they created a new schedule: they meet for ninety minutes every morning, starting today, Monday.

A more significant clue is the list of attendees. Other than Hix, three high-calibre researchers from his own organisation are in attendance, along with the directors of Wyeleigh, Northkirk and Stanmoor, the last of whom is Jason Lo, the Organisation’s chief of Antimemetics and Hix’s direct manager.

He steals a glance at the car parked behind him. There’s also this one. Hix doesn’t know for sure who the limo belongs to, but the list of people in the world who have the authority to drive a street vehicle into an Organisation containment building is extremely short. Someone from the C-suite is interested enough in their covert discussions to show up in person. This is a new and non-trivially alarming development.
He nods at the car. ‘Shouldn’t this place be lousy with private security right now?’
Fletcher shrugs.
‘Anybody go into the unit with the C? Bodyguard? Anybody stay in the car?’
‘No.’

Hix glances at the car again. The windows are tinted, though surely there’s a driver behind the wheel, at least. But where’s the real protection? Maybe it’s all invisible. Microbes. Occult spells of warding. He feels like the car is watching him back.
‘Open your mouth, please.’ Fletcher puts a disclike cap on Hix’s head, presses an emitter to the roof of his mouth, and fires two pulses of radiation through his brain. ‘Any psychic intrusions?’
Muffled by the emitter, Hix manages, ‘Uh-uh.’
She pulls out the emitter and discards it. ‘Did you experience REM sleep in the past twelve hours?’
He wipes his mouth. ‘Yes.’
‘How many digits do you have?’
‘Ten.’
‘Count them for me, please.’
Hix spreads his fingers and counts them. ‘One, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten.’ His right thumb is ‘five.’

Fletcher injects him with a substance that will prevent his body from rejecting the germ, then lifts a germ out of one of the cages. It splays its tendrils out, confused about being picked up. ‘Tilt your head back and look at the ceiling, please. Eyes wide open. And, if you could take off your glasses.’
Hix obliges, handing his glasses to Fletcher for scanning. ‘I dislike this part,’ he states.

Fletcher has no comment. She lays the germ over his eyes, like a sleep mask. There’s a cold, sludgy sensation as it wraps itself around his chin and hair, then the tendrils meet behind his neck and begin knitting with his spine. Hix sees darkness for a few worrying seconds, then a circular indentation forms in the germ’s hide over the top of his right eye, and there’s a feeling like part of his brain dislocating, and a fake eye opens where his real one would be. The fake eyeball is around four times the size of his own. Though it is singular, its four pupils grant him decent depth perception, and he can see a little way into the ultraviolet.

The germ was originally part of this C. gigantes specimen’s gut fauna. The stillborn creature had a small but flourishing population of them, and the Organisation managed to save them for study. The germ, whose neurological layout has some disturbing similarities to a human’s, is acting as an external block of short-to-medium-term memory, and as a proxy between the conscious Ed Hix and the real world. When the meeting is over, the germ will be removed and incinerated, along with all memory of the meeting.

There are other amnestic approaches – gas, injectable drugs, surgical techniques, occult rituals. These are safe, proven technologies for mass use on the general public and Organisation staff alike, but they all operate on the same essential principle that the unwanted knowledge has already entered the mind and must now be removed or suppressed after the fact. Such procedures are imperfect. Memory removal can leave critical fragments behind, occasionally enough for people to rebuild dangerous wholes; and mnestic technologies for causing suppressed memories to reassert themselves are continually advancing. The Class Y family of biomnestics is promising to render all after-the-fact memory erasure techniques irrelevant. The only amnestic defence against the completed Class Y will be decapitation. So, if there’s advance warning time, it’s better to physically compartmentalise, to airgap; to outsource the memories to another organism entirely and never let them touch your own mind. You can’t be forced to recall something that you genuinely never experienced.
Class Z carries an entirely different class of problems with it, of course.

It’s a complex and dynamic field, one of several fields in which Hix is a world expert. There are machines that could perform the same task as the germ, silicon modules you wear like a headset, plugged into a surgically implanted jack behind your ear, but Hix would rather die than submit to interfacing his brain directly with a computer, especially an Organisation-made computer. Nobody is getting his brain waves. When he joined the Organisation, thirty years ago, he put a DNU in his will – Do Not Upload. Everybody thought he was crazy.
Of course, using both the germs and a Vegas room feels rather like overkill. That’s another clue.

‘Your belongings have been scanned,’ Fletcher tells him. He refills his pockets and takes up his laptop. Walking slightly unsteadily because of the new weight he’s carrying on his head, he climbs the stairs to the airlock.

Hix would be the first to admit that a typical Organisation staff member has appalling taste. A typical Organisation staff member picks brutal functionality over aesthetic pleasure one hundred times out of one hundred, and a depressing percentage of Organisation staff don’t even comprehend the distinction. Hix sees this reflected in the architectural choices and interior design of the Organisation’s buildings and offices, and in its labs and containment facilities, which commonly cultivate a hopeless, bleak-cliff-edge atmosphere. He sees it in its machinery, its devices, its tools and even its font choices. Hard edges, clashing colours, failing aircon, impersonality, clutter, claustrophobia.

And so, STM-00-1006’s interior is a surprise and a delight. Hix actually sighs. It seems like someone hired a designer. The place is spacious and modern, well lit, with select walls painted in bright secondary colours. There’s not a bit of exposed concrete in sight.

STM-00-1006 isn’t a single space but a self-contained suite laid out on two floors. There’s a central meeting area with a double-height ceiling, a long oval table and Herman Miller chairs. Along the left wall there are smaller breakout meeting rooms with frosted glass walls and doors. Above those, reached by a flight of stairs, there’s a kitchen area, and in the back are some additional rooms, restrooms and storage. The carpet is grey and orange, a non-repeating hexagonal pattern. The place is well ventilated, and smells of coffee.

Right inside the door, Marie Quinn is waiting for him, holding two steaming cups of coffee. She was recently made deputy chief of Antimemetics, and she and he go way back. She hands one of the coffees to him immediately as he steps in. He takes it, automatically. ‘Oh. Legendary. Thank y – Aah. Yes.’ He sees that she is wearing a germ on her head, just as he is. The effect is very disconcerting and he comes close to spilling his drink. ‘Aha. I try to prepare myself, but it’s always the first time, isn’t it?’
She asks, ‘How was China?’
‘Is there a point in making small talk? We’re just going to have the same small talk all over again the second we leave.’
‘Ed, a friendly conversation is its own reward.’

He sips his coffee. ‘Okay. Do you know Gödel’s first incompleteness theorem? In mundane terms, it states that there are some statements of mathematics that are true but can never be proven. Xie, who’s the UO China lead containment architect, was telling me that if you extend that concept from mathematics to the real world you get an assertion that some physical realities are just going to be there and scientifically unjustifiable. Inexplicabilities. Like there’s going to be a wall we hit one day, where all the remaining Unknowns are just going to be fixtures, permanently Unknowable, and we’ll have two options: give up and live our lives, or die trying and failing to know them. Which I thought was a thought-provoking take, because a Gödel sentence –’
‘Ed. Small talk?’
He stops. He starts over. ‘China was fine. How are you?’
‘I’m fine,’ she says.
‘Weren’t you also … uh, out? For a few weeks?’ Hix isn’t very good at remembering information about people.
‘Adam and I went to Italy. It was our anniversary.’
‘Great. How was Italy?’
‘Only had to fly back to take care of an emergency once,’ Quinn says, with a pained smile.

Hix spends a moment trying to figure out how to react to this, but before he can he is interrupted. There are three other people in the room, also wearing germs. Devlin, director of UO Northkirk, is at the main table, a very gangly and severe man, hunched over a laptop. And at the back of the room, Jason Lo is chatting with a man Hix does not know: Mahlo, director of the UO in the United Kingdom and Ireland.

He must be the C. It’s strange. In Hix’s mind, Cs are titans, epic men and women with authority blanketing the whole globe. But Mahlo is a basic little fellow of sixty or so, someone Hix wouldn’t look at twice, or even once, if he passed him in a corridor. Hix tries not to stare, but his own germ is extremely good at staring.
Mahlo catches his eye. ‘You’re here. Shall we begin?’
‘Ah. Yes … “sir”?’ Hix doesn’t know how to address him. ‘I’m guessing you’re all caught up.’

The nature of the asynchronous work loop is that the first part of any meeting in a Vegas room is spent reading notes left from prior meetings. Hix’s correct guess is that there was an hour-long pre-meeting, and then everybody broke for coffee, and now they’re resuming.

Mahlo takes a seat at the head of the table, with Devlin to his left. Lo sits to his right, and Quinn to Lo’s right. Mahlo indicates a particular vacant chair, opposite Quinn, where a printed document is waiting for Hix to read it.
Hix sets his laptop down, hesitant to approach the document. ‘You want me to read this now?’
‘Take as long as you need.’
‘Do we have quorum? Where’s my team?’ Hix asks. ‘We’re three bodies short.’

‘Read the document, Dr Hix,’ Mahlo says. He seems upbeat. Perhaps he’s projecting an upbeat facade to help Hix to forget exactly how much authority and power he wields. C-suites’ net worths are said to be essentially infinite. It’s not really about money at their level. Mahlo, and his kind, can do anything.
Hix sits, and reads.

The document is a scientific paper purportedly authored by Hix himself, with various of his fellow researchers co-authoring, including two who should be in this room now. Hix doesn’t recognise the paper’s title or content, but that’s nothing special in his line of work. The text is written in his own formal, academic style, so he has no reason to doubt its authenticity.

It’s a brisk read, very dense and to the point, written for a target audience of other memetics scientists. In the abstract, it announces the observation of a new, titanically powerful and dangerous (anti)memeplex, provisionally designated U-3125, for which the authors plan to seek Γ0 categorisation.
‘Hmm.’

The main body of the first page describes eight different phenomena, most but not all of them anomalous, most but not all of the anomalous ones controlled by the Organisation and having U-designations. From a cursory glance, the phenomena appear to be totally unrelated, either to one another or to the proposed U-3125. Hix suspects he could derive the implied link between them, given a few minutes, but elects to read on. He flips the piece of paper over. The whole document is just two sides of A4.

The other side is mostly mathematics. There is one graph, and one equation, and a brief technical description of two highly novel memeplectic transformation procedures, which the authors dub ‘amplification.’ Then there’s –

Something like a jump scare in text form. There is a crucial logical leap, and for Hix, the arrival of comprehension is so blunt, so sudden and frightening, that it physically startles him. Even knowing that Γ was on the table, even primed to expect something extremely nasty on this side of the paper, he recoils. He hisses to himself: ‘Oh, fucking hell.’
Nobody else says anything. They wait, expectantly, for Hix to gather his thoughts and draw some conclusions.

He reads the rest of the paper, figuring out what it’s going to say almost live as he reads it. As he reaches the end, the initial shock hasn’t worn off. The sheer scope of U-3125 is a significant distance beyond his current comprehension. He’s had a glimpse of it through a keyhole. He would need time in front of a computer to play with the results to get a grasp of it.
No. He needs to build filters first, the equivalent of lead-lined gloves, to let him manipulate this radioactive idea complex with some degree of safety. He feels like it may have glimpsed him.

Category Γ is reserved for highly destructive active Unknowns that are functionally impossible to contain – something past ε. There are exceedingly few of them. A Category Γ Unknown is an Unknown more or less guaranteed to ultimately end the world, no matter what is done to stop it. The only thing that can avert that particular scenario is if something else, likely some other Category Γ Unknown, ends the world first. Their relative threat levels are measured not in material containment resources but in inevitable years. The fewer, the worse. And, considered relative to the others he’s cleared for …
‘Yeah, this is it,’ he says. It’s bizarrely liberating. ‘This is the one that’s going to kill us.’ He looks around the table. ‘Did we obtain Gamma categorisation?’
‘No,’ Mahlo says.
‘No?’

Mahlo smiles thinly. ‘Current thinking in the C-suite is that Category Gamma is a confession of defeat. It’s bad for morale. It cultivates defeatist attitudes. Aside from the special categories, Epsilon is considered the top of the hierarchy as of right now. All extant Gammas are going to be reevaluated and recategorised Epsilon by the end of next year. Other than that, what do you think?’
Hix says, ‘You want a containment protocol? We’ve had this conversation a bunch of times before, correct?’
‘Let’s imagine this is the first time,’ Mahlo says.
Hix stares darkly at his paper. ‘We could exterminate all intelligent human life,’ he says. ‘If there are no sapient hosts in this universe, U-3125 can’t incarnate.’
There’s a faintly stunned pause. ‘Yes,’ Quinn says. ‘You’ve pitched that approach before. And I don’t think any of us here have ever been completely sure if you were serious.’

‘I’m completely serious that we could do it and completely serious that it would work,’ Hix says. ‘The Organisation motto is “Freedom means no fear.” If there are no humans, there is no fear. Job done.’
‘But also no freedom,’ Devlin says. He has a thin, reedy voice. ‘The dead aren’t free –’
‘We’re getting off topic,’ Quinn says. ‘We’re not exterminating all sapient life.’

‘We could immediately terminate and suppress all memetics and antimemetics research worldwide,’ Hix says. ‘Not just in the Organisation. We would have to systematically dismantle the whole scientific field, forever. Stop all the experiments, scrap all the research, brainwash all the researchers. If nobody actively researches this field, nobody will ever find U-3125. It stays buried in the farthest reaches of ideatic space indefinitely, like radioactive waste.’ He looks up at the ceiling. The problem is interesting. ‘Ironically, the most practical way to do that would be to develop an artificial meme. One that encodes the idea that memetics research is intrinsically worthless and harmful. Enrich it with religious or pseudoscientific virals and release it to the general public. A year after it got out we’d be tearing our own labs down. Unless the Antimemetics Division’s institutional immunity to that kind of external threat was strong enough to stand up to the pressure. Interesting scenario.
Even if we don’t go in that direction we should definitely think about war-gaming it in simulation, see what outcomes are likely –’
‘Ed,’ Quinn says.
‘No, hiding wouldn’t work. It could be introduced externally or occur naturally –’
‘We know. Ed, that’s already happened. U-3125 is incarnating as we speak. Look at these precursor anomalies. We’re in what you called the “foreshadow.” It’s here.’
Quinn is referring to predictive models that Hix must have created himself during prior meetings, models with which he doesn’t have time to familiarise himself. Still, he gets it.

He wishes he didn’t get it. His fear comes from a completely different place from most people’s. The sheer alien scale of the adversary is enough to intimidate most into submission. From a cursory read, U-3125 looks like a nightmare scenario; it’s going to turn human civilisation into something beyond Hix’s ability to imagine. But that’s every Monday in this job, and in any case Hix doesn’t have much of an imagination. He is intimately familiar with almost the entire Unknown database, and he is a world authority in anomalous containment. The few areas of science he doesn’t have genius-level ability in, he has trusted colleagues who do. Those are all solved problems, locked boxes.

This is different. He has more ideas, but there is, mechanically, no way to start working on the problem. It would eviscerate him the moment he tried to comprehend the entire problem. He would need to design and build the box while already inside the box he was building. He would need to box the universe.
He looks around the room’s walls. They seem to be holding up.

‘We could hide in units like this for the rest of our lives,’ he says. ‘Our whole species. While U-3125 roamed our reality unchecked, like a plague. Semantic inversion. I declare this to be the exterior of the containment unit. Done.’
No reaction.

‘I don’t think we can do it,’ he says. ‘If U-3125 is live in consensus nominality right now, the game is over. I don’t care if the Gamma category lives or dies, from where I’m sitting this Unknown is functionally uncontainable. I … my team and I may have said something different on prior iterations. I could be in the wrong headspace to see the answer. We are all of us different people from day to day.’
‘No,’ Mahlo says. ‘You say the same thing every time.’
‘So that’s it. Is that it?’

Mahlo says, ‘The Organisation was originally founded as a scientific research institution. And, although our responsibilities have expanded considerably in the centuries since then, I believe that, at our core, we are still a scientific research institution. In the majority of cases, this entails the establishment of formal containment protocols so that Unknowns can be kept safely, and indefinitely, and so that they can be studied. Standard guidance is against active neutralisation of Unknown phenomena and to avoid destruction at all costs. Everybody in this room is aware of this.

‘However. Senior Organisation officials such as me have the right to waive that guideline under certain narrow conditions. I am exercising that right. I deem that U-3125 cannot coexist with our world. We must destroy U-3125. Forever. Does that change your assessment?’
‘You want a neutralisation protocol,’ Hix deadpans. His expression is worsening by the minute.
Mahlo adds, ‘I know that neutralisation is … generally considered easier than mere containment.’
Hix says:

‘When I first joined the Organisation, I asked my mentor, who retired many years ago, “What’s the biggest Unknown we’ve ever contained?” That he was cleared for, I meant, of course. And he told me about a very old rumour he once heard, back in his earliest days, when he was just starting out. The rumour was that Abrahamic religions had not always been monotheistic. Originally, there were three capital-G Gods. And sometime in the past two hundred years, the Organisation had killed two of them.

‘I believed him. I was very young and inexperienced, and naïve, and kind of in awe. It wasn’t until years later that I thought back to the conversation – and the fact that I’d never heard that rumour, or anything like it, from anybody else – and realised he had been bullshitting me.

‘And now it’s decades later still, and modern memeplectic technology is a hundred times more advanced than it was back then, and I built thirty percent of it, and I look at what the Antimemetics Division handles on a quarterly basis, and I know better than anybody on the face of this Earth what is or is not possible, and …’

He trails off. They’re all waiting expectantly for him to say something. He can’t get there. He’s in the wrong frame of mind. Maybe he’s in denial, maybe the solution is an idea he doesn’t want to take on board. How ironic –
‘What did I say? Just tell me.’

‘Your team suggested that just because U-3125 is the most powerful memeplex ever observed doesn’t mean it’s the top of the hierarchy,’ Mahlo says. ‘You suggested that it would be possible to synthesise an idea an order of magnitude still more powerful than U-3125, specifically designed to neutralise it, and under our control. A countermeme.’

‘A bigger infinity. That would take … That could be … possible,’ Hix hazards. ‘It would be absurdly dangerous. It would require tremendous resources. And ten to twenty years of real-time work, completely uninterrupted. To avoid observation, we would need to be hermetically sealed away from the exterior universe for that entire time. We would need a lab as big as a Launch Arcology. Wait a second.’
His brain has just caught up. He realises the context in which he’s saying these things. And he’s been working for the Organisation for a long time.

‘It’s done,’ he says. ‘The lab, it’s been built. It was built decades ago, in secret, and we put our best researchers inside it and now the work is done. That’s what we’re meeting for, now. We’re ready to go. We’re figuring out how to deploy the countermeme. That’s brilliant! If I’m right, that’s brilliant. Am I right?’

‘Ed,’ Quinn says. ‘When you joined the Organisation, you were taught that a day would come when you would, with very little preparation, have to sacrifice much or all of your existence to protect what most needed protecting. You’ve worked here for thirty years. And all of that time you knew that that sacrifice would be in your future someday. We were all taught the same thing.’
It feels to Hix as if a shadow falls over him. He looks at Jason Lo, his director, who hasn’t spoken yet.

Lo says, ‘You’re right that the lab is built. Construction was completed in the last forty-eight hours. The construction crew have been amnesticised and dismissed. But the work hasn’t begun yet. That’s what today’s about.’
Hix says, ‘… That’s where my people are.’
‘That’s where your people are,’ Lo says. ‘They’re in the bunker, waiting. We have your cover story prepped. We’re faking your death. It’s time. You’re going under now.’
‘Now? No. No, I doubt that.’
‘Your team volunteered. I took care of them myself. They’re good people,’ Lo says.
‘Like hell,’ Hix says, ‘did I volunteer for this. You plotted this. While I was in China. Right? God, I bet that’s why you sent me to China in the first place. So you’d have space and time to plot.’
Quinn says, ‘Edward!’

Hix says, ‘No. Any prior version of me who agreed to this was a damned imbecile, and I disavow his opinions. This is a prison sentence. Verging on a life sentence. I refuse to spend twenty years not able to see the sky. I refuse to be entombed alive in work. I have …’
He trails off, and stares through the table, eyes defocused. He was about to say, ‘I have family.’
But he doesn’t.
He tries another tack. ‘This … has a low probability of success. The time frames are bad. U-3125 will be here by the end of the next decade –’
‘It has an excellent probability of success,’ Devlin says.
‘Define “excellent.”’
‘Better than fifteen percent. If it’s you.’ Devlin produces a thick report that presumably backs him up.
Hix peers at the document. He can see his own name on the cover. Goddammit. Fifteen percent is good. If he were anybody else in this room, he’d seize the chance with both hands.

Devlin goes on. ‘You convinced us that this had to be done. And that you had to be at the centre. You were prepared to make the sacrifice.’ He opens the document to a page in the back. The eyeball of his germ roves the page rapidly and finds the passage he wants. ‘Allow me to quote your own words to you: “U-3125 represents an omniversal-scale threat. It threatens alternate realisations of our cosmotype. It threatens microverses within our macroverse. It threatens universes that embed ours as fiction –”’

‘Go ahead and think of it as a prison sentence, if it helps,’ Mahlo interrupts. ‘Rescind your consent if you’d like. Go kicking and screaming. But the next place you’re going after this is the bunker.’
Hix glances around the room’s walls again. He makes it too obvious what he’s thinking.
‘The door’s locked, Dr Hix,’ Mahlo says. ‘You’re not exiting until we’re through here.’
‘What’s the cover story?’ Hix asks. ‘How were you planning to do it?’

‘An argon gas leak in STM-X02-370,’ Devlin explains. ‘The leak will be real. There’s a forged body in there already, a U-9133 special, impossible to distinguish from a real one. And we’ve tampered with your public schedule for the day. It puts you in that room, not this one. As for –’
‘He’s stalling,’ Mahlo says to Devlin and the others. ‘He doesn’t need to know any of this.’
‘Name somebody else,’ Lo suggests. ‘Being serious. Who in the world, other than you, stands a credible chance of solving this problem? Who could we send instead?’
Hix says nothing. There’s nobody. Really, nobody in the world. And he can do it.
Lo presses, ‘Is there anybody else? Even if they didn’t want to. Who has the skills we need, who isn’t already in the vault?’

The world shifts positions a little. Lo’s standing now. Quinn looks around alertly, gripping the arm of her chair. She has a fountain pen in her fist, uncapped. It’s like she just remembered something. Mahlo glances at Quinn, puzzled at her reaction to, apparently, nothing. Hix doesn’t notice anything.
‘It’s just me,’ Hix says.
‘It’s just you,’ Lo says. ‘That’s good enough for me.’
‘Hold on a second,’ Quinn says.
Lo pulls a gun out of nowhere. Hix’s germ’s enormous pupils shrink to violet pinpricks.

This is not part of any plan. Everybody in the room knows it. It’s a real gun. It’s impossible that he could have it. Quinn starts to rise out of her chair. Her own sidearm is locked in a box outside.

Lo aims at Ed Hix’s chest and fires twice. The first round pierces him in the lung. The second round, fired as Hix collapses, nicks his laptop screen, which is bulletproof, and ricochets up into the meeting room wall.

Lo turns, now aiming at Mahlo. He gets two more rounds off, each causing an ear-splitting electronic shriek and a flash of brilliant green light as Mahlo’s protective ward absorbs the energy. Quinn lunges at Lo from behind his gun arm, deflecting it upward with one hand while plunging the fountain pen into his throat with the other. Lo struggles. Quinn pulls hard, opening his throat all the way up. Lo’s fingers loosen and she spirits the gun away. Lo gurgles in agony and stumbles backward, clutching futilely at his wound. He smashes his head – the germ he’s wearing on his head – against a glass meeting room door, and slides down it into a spreading red lake. Quinn shoots him, once, through his head and germ, making sure he’s neutralised.
There are two seconds in which nothing happens.
Mahlo’s eyes meet Quinn’s. ‘Your thoughts?’ he asks.

‘Jason Lo was compromised, I don’t know how,’ Quinn says. She makes the gun safe, holsters it, and vaults over the table to check on Hix. He’s dead, she finds. Devlin is dead too. When in the hell did Devlin get hit? What just happened in this room? ‘This whole base could be compromised from top to bottom.’
‘Compromised when?’ Mahlo asks. ‘By whom? I thought U-3125 incinerated its victims. I thought it wasn’t survivable. But Lo was still high-functioning.’
‘We’ve miscalculated something,’ Quinn says.
‘Could there be a secondary form of the infection?’ Mahlo speculates. ‘If the U has a need for conscious exemplars –’

‘They could represent an exploitable vulnerability,’ Quinn says. ‘Something we could isolate. If we could work out a way of doing it while blind.’ She stands and backs away, running her hands through her hair, trying to focus. The whole plan is wreckage. They need to start over and she doesn’t know where to start from. ‘We just … lost … Hix was crucial. He was our best option.’
Mahlo asks, ‘Do we have alternate options?’

Quinn says nothing. She has inklings. She doesn’t dare voice them, in case the act of speaking the ideas destroys them. What if someone else like Lo is listening to this, somehow? What if Mahlo himself becomes compromised? They got this far using discipline and structure, by containing their plan inside a sealed unit and destroying their own memories of it every time they left. That was working. But how can she execute any kind of plan without trust?
She looks desperately at Mahlo, and a lot of what she’s thinking crosses the gap without words, through her expression alone.
Mahlo is calm. ‘The Organisation no longer believes in unsolvable problems,’ he says. ‘Congratulations on your promotion to chief of Antimemetics. Tell me what you need.’
‘I need time,’ she says. ‘To think.’
As she is thinking, she and Mahlo realise that there is a faint new sound in the room, a kind of electrical fizzing.

She turns around. It’s coming from the airlock. There is a panel of controls beside it. The fizzing rapidly intensifies, culminating in a loud, sharp electrical discharge that causes the controls to go dark. After a few seconds, some kind of viscous, glossy liquid begins flowing out from the lower edge of the panel. It looks like machine oil. The fluid spreads across the floor.
And the inner airlock door begins to whirr open.
Quinn opens the panel. The machinery and wiring inside are an illegible mess. ‘The hydraulics have been sabotaged. Lo must have smuggled tools in as well as the gun –’
‘It’s mechanically impossible for both airlock doors to open simultaneously, is that correct?’ Mahlo asks.

‘It was,’ Quinn says. She hurries to the back of the room, where a medical kit is mounted on the wall. She throws most of the kit aside, keeping only a strangely shaped capsule with a thin nozzle and pink fluid inside.
‘What happens if both doors open?’
‘Unclear.’
‘U-3125 is out there,’ Mahlo says.

‘I know.’ Quinn applies the capsule to her right wrist and infuses the first half of the dose. It’s fast-acting chemical amnestic. She hopes that splitting one dose between the two of them will be enough.
The inner door finishes opening fully. A few hopeless seconds pass, and then, inevitably, the outer door begins to open too.

For Quinn, it is as if a suffocating wave rolls over her, pouring into the containment unit from outside. She feels seen, in the worst way. She feels caught in a hostile spotlight, pinned, guilty. In her body, she senses an angered vibration, a kind of wrathful engine ramping up in response.
Outside, Fletcher stands up from her terminal, puzzled. She has never seen the interior of the containment unit before. She calls out to Quinn and Mahlo, ‘What’s happening in there?’
‘Stay where you are,’ Quinn orders her.
‘I feel something,’ Mahlo says. ‘In my head. My germ, I should say.’

‘Me too. Roll up your sleeve. You also need to deactivate your shield for a second.’ He obliges, and Quinn gives him the rest of the drug. Quinn sorely wishes the shields were standard-issue, but they are exceptionally hard to come by, and there are serious controversies and side effects associated with them.
Fletcher is coming up the airlock steps. There’s something in her hand. A gun. Quinn’s. It was supposed to be locked away.
‘We have a major containment breach,’ Quinn tells her. ‘Don’t come in here. Return to your post.’
Fletcher isn’t listening. She says, ‘There’s a X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​X​ in your mind.’

Quinn knew that U-3125 ruined minds. She didn’t know it could do it this fast. She says, ‘Ms Fletcher, I’m armed. If you step inside this unit, I am going to shoot you. I’m serious, get back. Last chance. Fuck!’
Fletcher raises her weapon. Quinn kills her. She falls backward. She bumps down the steps, most of the way to the bottom.
In the distance, a base-wide containment alarm starts up.
Quinn says to Mahlo, ‘We need to get you out of here. Then we need to sterilise the base.’

‘There’s a contingency warhead,’ Mahlo says. ‘Code name DRY ERASE.’ He clutches his head. His germ is twitching unhappily and changing colour and texture, as if an infection is spreading across its pale grey skin.
